text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Validation helps departments decide whether an AI system performs well enough, for the right users and populations, before it is piloted, scaled, or sustained.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Validation helps departments decide whether an AI system performs well enough, for the right users and populations, before it is piloted, scaled, or sustained.
textPreview: Validation helps departments decide whether an AI system performs well enough, for the right users and populations, before it is piloted,...

text: Validation protects agencies from scaling systems that are inaccurate, inequitable, poorly integrated, or untrusted by staff and communities.
textPreview: Validation protects agencies from scaling systems that are inaccurate, inequitable, poorly integrated, or untrusted by staff and communit...

text: A model that performs well in a demo can fail in local workflows, underrepresented groups, or changing conditions.
textPreview: A model that performs well in a demo can fail in local workflows, underrepresented groups, or changing conditions.

text:
Use this slide in the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
Emphasize the core guardrail: A model that performs well in a demo can fail in local workflows, underrepresented groups, or changing conditions.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Model Validation and Evaluation Basics in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Validation is the disciplined process of checking whether an AI-supported workflow is accurate, reliable, safe, equitable, usable, and appropriate for its intended public health purpose. It should be understandable to nontechnical staff because validation results inform governance decisions.

Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

Purpose fit: whether the system solves the public health problem it is intended to support. Performance: accuracy, false positives, false negatives, completeness, reliability, and stability. Equity: subgroup performance, missing voices,.

text: Pilot evaluation should compare the AI-supported workflow to the current process. Monitoring should continue after deployment because models, data, staff behavior, guidance, and populations change. Governance should define thresholds for.

text: Define acceptance criteria before testing begins. Use realistic examples and local workflow conditions. Document limitations and do not scale until governance reviews evaluation evidence.

Use this slide in the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with What Validation Should Cover, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
What would count as acceptable performance for this workflow? Which groups or locations need subgroup review? What evidence should governance require before scale-up?

text: Design a validation plan for one proposed AI use case, including test examples, performance measures, subgroup checks, human review expectations, and go/no-go criteria.

Use this slide in the Model Validation and Evaluation Basics training module.
Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47.
Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Validation plan outline, Evaluation metric list, Governance decision thresholds.
Use this topic with Plays 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 3, 14, 40, 41, 42, 44, 47. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
